An LLM used as the author of a corpus, deciding content rather than just wording, contracts toward a narrow set of modes, genres, and scenarios, compounding across generated documents. Observed empirically as extreme repetitiveness across text types.

When the LLM both decides what a document is about and how to phrase it, sampling repeatedly from the same instruct model concentrates on its highest-probability content patterns (genre gravity), and each generated document can reinforce the pattern for the next through shared prompt exemplars (recursive collapse). Diversity fixes at the prompting level, such as more axes or rotating exemplars, only partially offset this.

This is treated as a structural limit of LLM authorship, not a fixable prompting bug: an LLM sampling repeatedly for content decisions has an effectively smaller reachable content space than the nominal corpus size suggests, independent of the good-faith diversity engineering already applied (registers, forms, exemplar rotation, seed vocabulary). The proposed resolution demotes the LLM from author to stylist (node 38): a non-LLM program decides content structure and a persistent world-state; the LLM only renders local fragments into fluent prose, which removes the LLM's ability to make content-level choices that concentrate. The concern recurred in a new form on the first grounded run (node 51): with the program as author, contraction entered through worldgen’s single artisan vocabulary rather than through the LLM’s preferences, collapsing every register into one world. The remedy is programmatic breadth, the eight domain lexicons of node 52, not a return to LLM-authored content.
